data.title: RocketDNA Expands Data Service Partnership with Vault Minerals at King of the Hills Gold Operation
data.description: Drone technology services company RocketDNA has secured an 18-month contract extension valued at $705,000 with Vault Minerals to provide ongoing integrated aerial data services at the King of the Hills gold operation in Western Australia. The 18-month agreement — signed between RocketDNA’s WA division and Vault subsidiary Greenstone Resources (WA) — will expand the existing contract to include RocketDNA’s xBot remote and fully-autonomous drone capabilities and automated data processing workflows. Vault will deploy the cutting-edge technology alongside RocketDNA’s proprietary SiteTube platform, with operational support delivered onsite and remotely from teams in Perth and Adelaide. Contract Extension RocketDNA chief executive officer Christopher Clark said the company was pleased to have secured an extension to its existing contract with Vault. “This reflects Vault’s confidence in our service and marks a milestone in integrating remote autonomous operations into daily mining workflows at King of the Hills,” he said. King of the Hills has been a proving ground for xBot hardware, and Vault an early adopter of the SiteTube platform. “The value-add from these technologies has culminated in this long-term commitment, and the addition of our automated data processing workflow has further strengthened our long-standing partnership with Vault,” Mr Clark said. Estimated Revenue The new contract will have an estimated annual recurring revenue (ARR) of $470,000, with different components based on usage rates. The estimated total contract value and ARR assume utilisation of all services at current levels, delivered under individual purchase orders at the agreed-upon contract pricing and also incorporates a 90-day termination notice period. Vault Minerals is an intermediate gold producer with operations in Australia and Canada. Its key assets in WA include the Mount Monger, Deflector and Leonora developments (comprising the King of the Hills open pit and underground mines and processing facility, along with the Darlot underground mine), while in Canada the company owns and operates the Sugar Zone gold project.

data.title: BOJ Should Stand Ready to Hike Rates, Board Member Takata Says — Update
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

The Bank of Japan should be ready to resume policy tightening if U.S. trade talks progress, policy board member Hajime Takata said, confirming that the bank is still looking to raise interest rates.

"I believe that the bank is currently only pausing its policy interest rate hike cycle and should continue to make a gear shift after a certain period of 'wait-and-see,'" Takata said in a speech to business leaders in Mie prefecture, central Japan.

"Given that uncertainties regarding various U.S. policies remain high, I believe that the bank is required to conduct monetary policy in a more flexible manner without being too pessimistic," he said on Thursday.

Economists are split on when the central bank will raise rates next. Some think the BOJ could act in the autumn once there's more clarity on trade negotiations. Others say it won't hike at all this year.

Amid tariff uncertainty, the BOJ has maintained interest rates unchanged at 0.5% since its last increase in January. The bank is scheduled to hold its next meeting on July 30-31.

Many economists expect the BOJ to stand pat again this month because Japan hasn't been able to reach a trade deal with the U.S.

President Trump has recently stepped up pressure on Japan, complaining that it isn't buying enough American cars and rice.

Japan has said there are some areas where the two countries' understandings don't align. Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba said Wednesday that Japan is willing to cooperate with the U.S. to balance trade between the two countries, but will only buy American products that meet its national interests and standards.

With higher tariffs expected to cause a deceleration in the U.S. economy, the BOJ must be careful about the risk that a divergence in monetary policy could cause volatility in financial markets, particularly for foreign-exchange rates, Takata said.

Historical trends show the BOJ has typically followed the U.S. in implementing rate cuts, he added.

"If the Federal Reserve resumes its rate cuts, that may reduce the flexibility of the bank's [BOJ] monetary policy," Takata said.

Still, a serious recession in the U.S. is unlikely, the BOJ board member said. Trump's economic policies have up and downsides, he said, with some measures like tax cuts and deregulation having the potential to boost economic growth.

Looking at Japan's economic conditions, Takata highlighted that the BOJ's inflation target is close to being achieved. The bank should keep adjusting the degree of monetary accommodation if positive corporate behavior, including pay raises, is confirmed, he said.

The BOJ's quarterly tankan corporate survey earlier this week showed that sentiment among large manufacturers improved in the second quarter, despite concerns over the impact of higher U.S. tariffs.

"I would like to closely monitor whether the momentum toward achieving the price stability target, which has finally started to operate, will not be dampened by U.S. tariff policy," Takata said.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: Australia's Wia Gold shines on gold find in Namibia project
data.description:
** Shares of Australia's Wia Gold Ltd rise 10.9% to A$0.255, their highest level since June 16

** Gold miner rose as much as 14% to A$0.2625, marking its biggest intraday pct gain since June 12

** WIA identifies highest concentration of gold mineralization to date at its Kokoseb gold project in Namibia

** Co says drilling results could grow site operations significantly, and will announce resource estimates later this month

** Around 11.5 million shares change hands, over 8x the 30-day average of about 1.35 million

** Shares up 75.9% YTD

data.title: U.S. Market Rally on Vietnam Deal Shows More Relief Than Celebration — Market Talk
data.description:
0354 GMT - The U.S. market rally overnight after a trade deal with Vietnam was likely more a sense of relief rather than a celebration, Varathan Vishnu of Mizuho Securities writes in a note. The deal is lopsided, and the all-in 20% tariff on Vietnam isn't insignificant. While that alone should have led to risk-off sentiment among exporters, the number is well below the 46% threatened earlier. This provided relief to U.S. companies that rely heavily on Vietnam factories and imports, Vishnu says. This doesn't mean optimism will reign among other Asian countries as the deal is "a stark reminder of the imbalance in leverage, to the disadvantage of Asian exporters," and the risk that the U.S. can "unilaterally change the rules," Vishnu adds. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

2306 GMT - Xponential Fitness's announcement that the SEC had wrapped up its investigation without taking action removes a major overhang on the stock, Jefferies analysts led by Randal Konik say. The analysts expect investor sentiment to improve now that the uncertainty has cleared, and shares reflect that view as they are up 20% in after-hours trading. Xponential's shares, which closed at $7.46, trade at a deep discount compared with rival Planet Fitness, which closed at $106.83. That dislocation reflects a considerable upside potential for Xponential, the analysts say. "XPOF's valuation multiple has room to rise toward peer levels as the company executes on its growth plans," they say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

2253 GMT - Figma's filing to go public gives investors a window into the rapidly changing creative ecosystem, D.A. Davidson analysts say in a note. The maker of software used by designers has a strong financial profile, with sales up 48% year over year in the first quarter, shows artificial intelligence is accelerating the output of digital products and lowering the barrier to entry for new users, the analysts say. Those shifts should also benefit Adobe, and provide another data point to challenge the narrative that it will lose business as AI evolves, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

2229 GMT - Lululemon appears to be relying more and more on discounts as its products aren't resonating with shoppers, Jefferies analysts say after visiting brick-and-mortar stores. Throughout their visits, the analysts saw products that had gone through up to three rounds of markdowns, and discounts are starting to bleed into Lulu's core women's products. This trend is combined with the fact that Lulu's first-quarter inventory growth of 23% far outpaced its 7% sales growth. The analysts believe the retailer will have to lower its outlook because its current guidance only reflects modest declines. "Rising competition, weakening foot traffic, and elevated inventory levels are creating a perfect storm," they say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

2148 GMT - Cava has many of the same attributes that made Chipotle one of the fastest growing restaurant stocks since its IPO, Baird analysts say in a note. Among those are a straightforward operating model, which should yield higher foot traffic as it grows brand awareness with more locations, the analysts say. When Chipotle upped its store count from 200 to 500 locations, average store volumes climbed about 40%, which accelerated to 80% in the 10 years in which it grew to nearly 1,800 stores, the analysts say. Cava could head in a similar direction, considering its growth pattern has looked remarkably similar to Chipotle's early stages, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

2111 GMT - Lucid Group reports delivering 3,309 vehicles in 2Q and producing 3,863 vehicles. Both figures miss the estimates of Cantor analysts Andres Sheppard and Anand Balaji, who had been looking for 3,791 deliveries and 4,000 produced vehicles. Analysts polled by FactSet had been expecting Lucid to produce 5,300 vehicles during the quarter. The 3,863 tally is still an improvement from 2,110 in the same quarter a year earlier, the Cantor analysts say. They add that Lucid's guidance for producing 20,000 vehicles this year remains unchanged and in line with their own projections. Shares remain flat at $2.05 after hours. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

2004 GMT - The S&P 500 and Nasdaq set fresh highs, while the Dow Industrials stall as investors wait for the June jobs report ahead of the extended July 4th weekend. Energy shares led the gains as oil prices rose when Iran said it's suspending cooperation with the U.N. atomic agency, returning some of the risk premium that faded following last week's cease-fire. Health care stocks fall the most on big declines in a few health insurers on worries about data from plans on the Affordable Care Act exchanges. The DJIA falls 10 points to 44484, while the S&P 500 rises 0.5% to a record 6227 and the Nasdaq gains 0.9% to 20393. (patrick.sullivan@wsj.com)

1943 GMT - Treasury yields tick higher as markets wait for June's jobs report amid bets that the Fed isn't too far from cutting interest rates. Payrolls are expected to decline to 110,000 from 139,000, with the unemployment rate rising to 4.3% from 4.2% and wage gains slightly cooling, in a WSJ survey. Weekly jobless claims are forecast to increase a little. Markets price in a Fed hold this month, followed by a cut by a quarter of a percentage point in September. The 10-year rises 0.040 p.p. to 4.290% and the two-year gains 0.012 p.p. to 3.789%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1916 GMT - UniFirst executives note continued caution, which extends to both pricing and employment levels at its customers. CEO Steven Sintros says tariffs are prompting some of its suppliers to raise prices. "We're sort of stuck in the middle on that right now and it's unclear how that will play out," Sintros says on a 3Q earnings call. Meanwhile, customers are also "somewhat cautious" about adding workers and continuing to cut jobs as they have in recent quarters. The caution is likely weighing on shares, which fall 8.5%, even after the company reported better-than-expected quarterly results and backed its guidance for the year. (natalie.weger@wsj.com, @NatalieWeger)

1908 GMT - The dollar loses momentum ahead of an expected decline in U.S. job creation, as investors expect the Fed to start cutting interest rates in coming months. President Trump's announcement of a trade deal with Vietnam seems to contribute to dollar indexes falling from morning highs. Consensus for tomorrow's payrolls puts June's job creation at 110,000, down from May's 139,000. Some forecasters, however, expect much less. ADP says private-sector employers actually shed 33,000 jobs last month. The WSJ Dollar Index rises 0.1%, but is off earlier highs. The greenback strengthens around 0.1% versus the yen and the euro. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1825 GMT - Privately held fruit canner Del Monte Foods filed for bankruptcy after Tuesday's close. Shares of publicly traded Fresh Del Monte Produce fell 1.4% shortly after Wednesday's open before recovering, even though the two companies aren't connected. It's likely a case of mistaken identity by some traders. The similarly named entities were formerly both part of RJR Nabisco before KKR's leveraged buyout of the conglomerate in 1988, and were then spun off to divergent corporate fates. Fresh Del Monte, which sells fruit that is fresh, not canned, is in much better shape: profitable, with minimal long-term debt, a roughly $1.6 billion market cap and returns north of 50% over the past year. FDP shares have recovered to roughly flat Wednesday afternoon. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1824 GMT - The Trump administration's latest trade deal is welcome news for Nike investors. The deal lowers proposed tariffs on goods from Vietnam, where Nike sources much of its sneakers and apparel. The news removes some uncertainty on the sportswear giant's tariff-related cost increases, which has fueled investor fears more than the actual severity of tariff policies, BMO Capital Markets analyst Simeon Siegel says. "Before you can fix the problem, you need to diagnose it. Today felt like a step closer to diagnosis," he tells WSJ. Last week, the company had warned of a gross incremental cost increase of about $1B due to tariff rates in place. Nike climbs 4%. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: KOORUI Emerges as a Gaming Monitor Contender Ahead of Amazon Prime Day Surge
data.description:
LONDON, July 02, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — As Amazon Prime Day approaches, global demand for high-performance consumer electronics is once again on the rise. According to third-party data, electronics sales during Prime Day 2024 saw an 18% year-over-year increase, with gaming monitors and productivity devices among the top-performing categories.

*The G2411P features a 200Hz refresh rate and 1ms response time

In response to this momentum, emerging display brand KOORUI has announced a focused lineup of new products for Prime Day, including the gamer-optimized G2741L featuring Dual-Mode display technology. The entry-level high-refresh-rate G2411P, and the 34-inch curved ultrawide 34E6UC designed for immersive work and entertainment setups. A bundle featuring G2741L and G2411P will also launch globally, offering a versatile dual-screen solution. Meanwhile, the sleek E2412F caters to professionals and content creators seeking efficient and minimal design.

Founded on May 4, 2021, KOORUI was born with a clear mission: to enhance the human visual experience through high-quality, high-performance display products. The brand quickly expanded across major online platforms including Amazon, TikTok, Youtube and Walmart, supporting a wide range of use cases from gaming and work to content creation.

Behind KOORUI lies a strong foundation of industrial and supply chain expertise, drawing on the capabilities of one of China’s leading display manufacturing enterprises. While it operates independently as a consumer-facing brand, this technical backing empowers KOORUI to deliver products that balance innovation, quality, and scalability.

Industry analysts see KOORUI as a representative of a new generation of tech brands going global. Its scenario-driven product strategy—centered around user context rather than specs alone—offers a differentiated approach in a crowded display market.

“Prime Day is not just a sales moment, but a global stage for brand visibility,” note observers. For KOORUI, it’s a chance to link product innovation with global user recognition, while subtly showcasing the growing strength of Chinese display technology in the international arena.

About KOORUI

Powered by HKC’s semiconductor display expertise, KOORUI blends innovative panel manufacturing with human-centered product design. Its monitor lineup empowers creators, gamers, and professionals around the globe with reliable, high-performance visual solutions.

Press Contact:

KOORUI PR Team

Email: hkcprcontact@szhk.com.cn

Website:

https://www.koorui.net/

https://www.hkcglobal.net/

A photo accompanying this announcement is available at https://www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/b7a79ad8-1917-4dac-8272-64dd7695a7ae

data.title: Late House Drama Holds Up Trump Megabill — WSJ
data.description:
By Olivia Beavers, Richard Rubin and Lindsay Wise

WASHINGTON — House Republican leaders hit a wall Wednesday night after trying all day to win over GOP critics of President Trump's sprawling tax-and-spending bill, with the party's narrow majority again creating last-minute drama on Capitol Hill.

Four GOP lawmakers — Keith Self of Texas, Victoria Spartz of Indiana, Andrew Clyde of Georgia and Brian Fitzpatrick of Pennsylvania — voted with Democrats against a procedural "rule" vote, blocking the party, at least for the moment, from proceeding to final passage. The rule vote was still open into the night as leaders tried to cajole colleagues to change their votes.

Dozens of lawmakers had raised complaints about the revised "big, beautiful bill," which passed the Senate on Tuesday, with fiscal hawks wanting deeper spending reductions and moderates worried about cuts to the social safety net. GOP attendance issues and continued talks stalled action late into Wednesday, with the rule vote providing the first real test of support.

"It's been a good day. We're in a good place right now," said House Speaker Mike Johnson (R., La.), who met with lawmakers all day and into the night. "We are going to deliver the big beautiful bill."

Earlier in the day, one noncontroversial procedural vote was held open for more than seven hours — believed to be the longest in House history — as discussions continued with holdouts. On Truth Social, Trump exhorted Republicans to move the bill forward. Minutes later, the rule vote began, with Johnson effectively daring dissidents to vote against it.

When the late-night vote stalled, Johnson signaled he was prepared to wait. "I'll keep it open as long as it takes to make sure we've got everybody here and accounted for and all the questions answered," he said shortly before 11 p.m. on Fox News.

Past standoffs have been resolved following pressure campaigns by the president and party leaders. Given Republicans' thin 220-212 majority, the GOP can't advance a party-line bill if more than three House Republicans join Democrats in opposition.

"I'm not there yet," said Chip Roy (R., Texas), a member of the conservative House Freedom Caucus. "We got to understand what the steps are to deal with how the Senate bill came up short," he said. He declined to comment on whether such steps could include executive orders from the president.

Trump wants the bill on his desk this week to meet his self-imposed July 4 deadline, though there are no practical consequences from waiting.

Trump met separately Wednesday with members of the House Freedom Caucus and moderate Republicans, and urged both groups to get on board, according to White House officials. He emphasized the tax cuts in the legislation, and he sought to play down concerns about Medicaid cuts, saying the proposed reductions to the federal-state health program for low-income and disabled people would target waste and fraud.

Some critics started to come around.

"Big day, we hope this all works out," said Rep. Tim Burchett (R., Tenn.) as he left the White House after what he called a "very good conversation" with Trump and Vice President JD Vance. Burchett has sought deeper deficit reductions.

Rep. Morgan Griffith (R., Va.), a member of the House Freedom Caucus, said that the president's approach involved more of a soft touch rather than arm twisting.

Rep. Thomas Massie (R., Ky.), a deficit hawk who has clashed publicly with Trump, remained a firm "no" on the bill. Massie was one of two "no" votes, along with Rep. Warren Davidson (R., Ohio), on the initial version of the House bill back in May.

But Davidson said Wednesday he would back the revised legislation. "This is probably the best product we can get," he said.

Spartz had pledged to vote "no" on the procedural rule, citing what she called broken commitments from Johnson. But she said she would vote "yes" on final passage.

Rep. Don Bacon (R., Neb.), a retiring lawmaker who is one of three House Republicans from districts Trump lost, said he would vote for the bill because of the tax cuts and military spending included in it, even though there are portions he doesn't like.

If the House altered or failed to approve the Senate measure, Trump's July 4 deadline probably wouldn't be met, as senators have left town.

Skeptics on Capitol Hill said they have seen this film before: Fiscal conservatives and other Republican holdouts say they can't support a bill, only for most of them to fall in line when Trump gets directly involved.

Rep. Ralph Norman (R., S.C.), a member of the House Freedom Caucus, voted early Wednesday morning against moving the bill out of the House Rules Committee, joined by Roy. The panel debated, then narrowly advanced the bill to the House floor.

"This bill's a nonstarter," he said. But by Wednesday night, he said he had been convinced to back the package, declining to provide specifics. "We found out we've things that were going to happen which will affect the whole country in a good way," he said.

Democrats have stayed united in opposition, saying the bill cuts Medicaid and other programs for lower-income people to pay for tax cuts for the wealthy.

"That so many of you are blindly going along with this just to please the guy in the White House is shameful," Rep. Jim McGovern (D., Mass.), the top Democrat on the Rules Committee, said to his GOP colleagues.

The Senate passed the bill on Tuesday, following an 27-hour marathon of amendment votes. The legislation funds Trump's priorities including the extension of his 2017 tax cuts; no tax on tips and overtime; and a large funding boost for the president's immigration and border policies.

The House GOP is being asked to digest a series of changes that were made to a version of the bill that passed the lower chamber weeks earlier by one vote.

The House Freedom Caucus released a three-page list of what it called failures in the Senate bill. Those include a 12-month runway for wind and solar projects to start construction and still get tax credits, and its violation of the House framework that limited tax cuts unless Republicans also approved spending cuts.

Given the tough math, Johnson repeatedly warned the Senate against altering the House bill. In passing the measure back in May, Johnson had wrangled conservatives who were pushing for spending cuts and centrists who were warning against steep changes to programs such as food stamps and Medicaid.

The Senate version's deeper policy shifts on Medicaid would leave 12 million people without insurance by 2034, according to the Congressional Budget Office, compared with 11 million people in the House version.

While both bills aim to quickly phase out clean-energy tax credits for solar and wind companies, the Senate version would have slightly more lax requirements as to when a company can claim the tax credit. The Senate bill would raise the debt ceiling by $1 trillion more than the House's proposed $4 trillion.

The Senate version would have a more substantial impact on the U.S. deficit, according to the CBO. It would add $3.4 trillion to the nation's debt over a decade, compared with the House bill, which would add $2.4 trillion, the nonpartisan budget scorekeeper found in an updated score.

Write to Olivia Beavers at Olivia.Beavers@wsj.com, Richard Rubin at richard.rubin@wsj.com and Lindsay Wise at lindsay.wise@wsj.com

data.title: CR Double-Crane Pharma's Unit Gets Nod to Trial Dry Eye Drug
data.description:
China Resources Double-Crane Pharmaceutical's unit, China Resources Zizhu Pharmaceutical, will conduct clinical trials for the J002 drug after obtaining the approval of the Chinese drug administration, according to a Shanghai bourse filing on Thursday.

The drug will be tested as a treatment for dry eye.

The pharmaceutical company invested 15.6 million yuan into the research and development of the drug.

CR Double-Crane Pharma's shares rose over 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Solstice Minerals Starts Induced Polarisation Survey at Western Australia Copper-Gold Project; Shares Down 4%
data.description:
Solstice Minerals said that it started an induced polarization geophysical survey at its Nanadie Well copper-gold project in Western Australia, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

The survey is designed to test the immediate northern and southern strike extensions of the project's mineral resource estimate.

The results of the survey will help the firm prioritize extension targets already identified in an ongoing geological review and firm up collar locations for a maiden reverse circulation drilling program planned for the third quarter, the filing said.

Solstice Minerals' shares fell 4% in recent trading on Thursday.

data.title: Siemens Recently Notified By U.S. Commerce Department That Export Control Restrictions On EDA Software & Technology To Customers In China No Longer In Place
data.description:
Siemens AG :

  • SIEMENS - RECENTLY NOTIFIED BY U.S. COMMERCE DEPARTMENT, THAT EXPORT CONTROL RESTRICTIONS ON EDA SOFTWARE & TECHNOLOGY TO CUSTOMERS IN CHINA NO LONGER IN PLACE

  • SIEMENS: RESTORED FULL ACCESS TO SOFTWARE, TECHNOLOGY CLASSIFIED UNDER EXPORT CONTROL CLASSIFICATION NUMBERS (ECCNS) 3D991 AND 3E991

data.title: Australia's Boss Energy sinks after Jefferies downgrade to 'hold', stock biggest loser on benchmark
data.description:
** Shares of Boss Energy fall as much as 8.5%, their biggest intraday pct loss since April 7

** BOE is the biggest percentage loser on the benchmark S&P/ASX 200

** Jefferies downgrades the uranium miner to "hold" from "buy", cuts PT to A$5 from A$5.40;

** Brokerage lowers co's FY26 production outlook by 10% and increases unit costs by 23%, sees costs remaining elevated at 'Honeymoon' project in South Australia

** YTD, BOE up 71.8%

data.title: Oiltek International Subsidiary in Talks Over Sustainable Aviation Fuel Pilot Plant Program in Malaysia
data.description:
Oiltek International's subsidiary, Oiltek Sdn., is in discussions with SEDC Energy, a subsidiary of Sarawak Economic Development in relation to a sustainable aviation fuel pilot program in Sarawak, Malaysia, according to a Thursday filing with the Singapore Exchange.

As of now, no formal plans have been finalized and the parties have not entered into any definitive agreements.

data.title: Australia 10-Year Bond Yield Eases Ahead of RBA Decision
data.description:
Australia’s 10-year government bond yield fell toward 4.18% on Thursday, partially retracing the sharp rise seen in the previous session as investors positioned ahead of the Reserve Bank’s upcoming policy decision.

The central bank is widely expected to lower its 3.85% cash rate by 25bps next week, as economic growth remained weak and inflation risks have faded.

Adding to the dovish sentiment, Australia’s trade surplus on goods shrank to a near five-year low in May, as exports dropped while imports increased—signaling cooling external demand.

That followed a disappointing report on retail sales on Wednesday, which pointed to subdued domestic consumption.

The market also implies rates to drop further to a floor of 2.85%, taking it under most estimates of neutral.

data.title: CR Double-Crane Pharma Gets Registration Certificate for Compound Amino Acids Injection
data.description:
China Resources Double-Crane Pharmaceutical received a drug registration certificate for compound amino acids injection (18AA-IX) from the Chinese drug administration, according to a Shanghai bourse filing on Thursday.

The drug is used to supplement amino acids in patients with acute or chronic renal insufficiency who present with hypoproteinemia, malnutrition, or require supplementation before or after surgery.

The pharmaceutical company invested 10.7 million yuan into the research and development of the drug.

CR Double-Crane Pharma's shares rose over 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Boeing and Justice Department seek judge's approval for deal opposed by crash victims' families
data.description:
Key points:
  • Agreement allows Boeing to avoid being branded a convicted felon
  • Relatives of some of the 346 people killed in 737 MAX crashes in 2018 and 2019 objected to deal
  • Boeing to pay $1.1 billion in total under non-prosecution agreement

Boeing and the Justice Department on Wednesday asked a U.S. judge to approve an agreement that allows the company to avoid prosecution despite objections from relatives of some of the 346 people killed in two 737 MAX crashes in 2018 and 2019.

The deal enables Boeing to avoid being branded a convicted felon and to escape oversight from an independent monitor for three years that was part of a plea deal struck in 2024 to a criminal fraud charge that it misled U.S. regulators about a crucial flight control system on the 737 MAX, its best-selling jet.

Boeing argued the executive branch solely has the power to decide whether to bring or maintain a prosecution.

"Because it is entirely within the government’s discretion whether to pursue a criminal prosecution, an agreement not-to-prosecute does not require court approval," Boeing said, asking a judge to reject objections filed by the families and grant the government's motion to dismiss the charge.

"Disputing the government’s considered assessment of litigation risk, the calculation of the maximum fine, or the appropriate mechanism for compliance oversight, do not demonstrate - even remotely - that the government was clearly motivated by considerations contrary to the public interest."

The Justice Department said in a court filing it acted in good faith and in accordance with the law, agreeing to dismiss the case for an agreement "that secures a significant fine, compliance improvements, and a substantial victim compensation fund."

The families cited Judge Reed O'Connor's statement in 2023 that "Boeing's crime may properly be considered the deadliest corporate crime in U.S. history."

They argue dismissal is not in the public interest and obligations imposed on Boeing are not enforceable.

If the government declined to move forward with the prosecution even if the court rejected the deal, O'Connor should appoint a special prosecutor, the families said.

Boeing and the Justice Department both asked O'Connor to reject appointing a special prosecutor.

Under the deal, Boeing agreed to pay an additional $444.5 million into a crash victims fund to be divided evenly per crash victim, on top of a new $243.6 million fine.

Boeing in July 2024 agreed to plead guilty to a criminal fraud conspiracy charge after the two fatal 737 MAX crashes in Indonesia and Ethiopia.

Under the non-prosecution agreement, Boeing will pay $1.1 billion in total, including the fine, compensation to families and more than $455 million to strengthen the company’s compliance, safety and quality programs.

The vast majority of the families have settled civil suits with Boeing and collectively have been "paid several billion dollars," the Justice Department said.

data.title: Frasers Centrepoint Trust Issues SG$200 Million Worth of Bonds; Shares Down Under 1%
data.description:
Frasers Centrepoint Trust issued SG$200 million worth of 3.98% subordinated perpetual bonds comprised in Series 003, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange late Wednesday.

Shares of the trust were down under 1% in recent trading.

The bonds are under the SG$3 billion multicurrency debt issuance program.

OCBC or Oversea-Chinese Banking Corp was appointed as the sole lead manager for the Series 003 bonds which are expected to be listed and quoted on July 3.

data.title: Japan 30-year JGB auction bid-to-cover ratio 3.58 versus 2.92 at prior sale
data.description:
A sale of 30-year Japanese government bonds on Thursday had a bid-to-cover ratio of 3.58 versus 2.92 at a prior sale in June.

The auction's tail, another measure of demand, was at 0.31 yen versus 0.49 yen at the previous sale.

Benchmark JGB futures held declines after the sale.

($1 = 143.8100 yen)

data.title: Henlius Biotech Unit Receives EU GMP Certification for Two Biosimilars; Shares Gain 2%
data.description:
Shanghai Henlius Biotech said its wholly owned subsidiary, Henlius Biologics, has obtained good manufacturing practice compliance certificates from Belgium's Federal Agency for Medicines and Health Products, according to a Wednesday bourse filing.

Shares of the company were up about 2% in morning trade on Thursday.

The certification covers drug substance, drug product, and packaging lines for HLX11 and HLX14, which are biosimilars to pertuzumab and denosumab, respectively, and confirms their compliance with European Union GMP standards, the filing said.

data.title: Peak Minerals to Raise AU$3.5 Million via Share Placement; Shares Hit 52-Week High
data.description:
Peak Minerals received a firm commitment to raise about AU$3.5 million through the issue of 100 million shares to a European-based investor at AU$0.035 per share, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

The issue price is at par with the company's last closing price, and the new shares will be subject to a voluntary escrow period of one year from the date of issue, the filing said.

The funds will support the exploration at its Minta Rutile project in Cameroon, the filing added.

The placement is expected to settle on July 7, the company said.

Shares of the company rose past 11% in recent Thursday trade and earlier hit a 52-week high.

data.title: Worldline Begins Review of Risk Management Framework
data.description:
Worldline said Wednesday it launched a review of its risk management framework as part of its commitment to full transparency.

The French payment services company sought the assistance of two external firms for the review, which will focus on its remaining high brand risk portfolio.

Findings of the review are scheduled to be disclosed along with the group's earnings report on Oct. 21, with preliminary findings expected along with the fiscal first-half results on July 30.

data.title: EQT Unit Buys Four US Logistics Buildings
data.description:
EQT said Wednesday its real estate investment management arm, EQT Real Estate, purchased four logistics buildings in California, US.

The acquisition, carried out through EQT Exeter Industrial Value Fund VI, covered class-A assets with a combined area of 2 million square feet. The properties are fully leased to tenants from various industries, with a weighted average lease term of less than 3.5 years.

The Swedish private equity group noted that the properties feature modern bulk distribution specifications, including parking spaces for trailers and automotives.

data.title: India's Sensex Opens 0.2% Higher at 83540.74, Led by Auto, Tech Stocks
AUD/USD's Upward Momentum Starting to Increase, Chart Shows — Market Talk
data.description: AUD/USD's upward momentum is starting to increase after it rebounded sharply from a low of 0.6373, says Quek Ser Leang of UOB Global Economics & Markets Research in a note. The currency pair is trading not far below a channel top, which is presently at 0.6595 on the daily chart, the senior technical strategist notes. If AUD/USD were to break and hold above 0.6595, it could potentially extend gains toward 0.6685, the strategist says. However, a break below support at 0.6480 would nullify the currency pair's upside potential, the strategist adds. AUD/USD is 0.2% lower at 0.6572. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: BOJ Should Stand Ready to Hike Rates, Board Member Takata Says — Update
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

The Bank of Japan should be ready to resume policy tightening if U.S. trade talks progress, policy board member Hajime Takata said, confirming that the bank is still looking to raise interest rates.

"I believe that the bank is currently only pausing its policy interest rate hike cycle and should continue to make a gear shift after a certain period of 'wait-and-see,'" Takata said in a speech to business leaders in Mie prefecture, central Japan.

"Given that uncertainties regarding various U.S. policies remain high, I believe that the bank is required to conduct monetary policy in a more flexible manner without being too pessimistic," he said on Thursday.

Economists are split on when the central bank will raise rates next. Some think the BOJ could act in the autumn once there's more clarity on trade negotiations. Others say it won't hike at all this year.

Amid tariff uncertainty, the BOJ has maintained interest rates unchanged at 0.5% since its last increase in January. The bank is scheduled to hold its next meeting on July 30-31.

Many economists expect the BOJ to stand pat again this month because Japan hasn't been able to reach a trade deal with the U.S.

President Trump has recently stepped up pressure on Japan, complaining that it isn't buying enough American cars and rice.

Japan has said there are some areas where the two countries' understandings don't align. Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba said Wednesday that Japan is willing to cooperate with the U.S. to balance trade between the two countries, but will only buy American products that meet its national interests and standards.

With higher tariffs expected to cause a deceleration in the U.S. economy, the BOJ must be careful about the risk that a divergence in monetary policy could cause volatility in financial markets, particularly for foreign-exchange rates, Takata said.

Historical trends show the BOJ has typically followed the U.S. in implementing rate cuts, he added.

"If the Federal Reserve resumes its rate cuts, that may reduce the flexibility of the bank's [BOJ] monetary policy," Takata said.

Still, a serious recession in the U.S. is unlikely, the BOJ board member said. Trump's economic policies have up and downsides, he said, with some measures like tax cuts and deregulation having the potential to boost economic growth.

Looking at Japan's economic conditions, Takata highlighted that the BOJ's inflation target is close to being achieved. The bank should keep adjusting the degree of monetary accommodation if positive corporate behavior, including pay raises, is confirmed, he said.

The BOJ's quarterly tankan corporate survey earlier this week showed that sentiment among large manufacturers improved in the second quarter, despite concerns over the impact of higher U.S. tariffs.

"I would like to closely monitor whether the momentum toward achieving the price stability target, which has finally started to operate, will not be dampened by U.S. tariff policy," Takata said.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: Mercialys Signs Third Lease for French Property with Lidl
data.description:
Mercialys secured a third lease for its property in Niort, France, from German food retailer Lidl.

Lidl will launch in the second half of 2026, according to a Wednesday release. The premise was previously occupied by hypermarket chain Géant Casino.

Additionally, the retail real estate company is in advanced negotiations with two other retail companies not yet established in the region.

data.title: China 10-Year Yield Steady
data.description:
China’s 10-year government bond yield held steady at 1.64% on Thursday, as investors assessed the latest PMI figures.

The Caixin China General Composite PMI returned to expansion territory, reaching its highest level since March.

This improvement was largely driven by a renewed increase in manufacturing activity, which helped offset a slowdown in the services sector that fell to a nine-month low.

Earlier in the week, official PMI readings offered a slightly different view: the manufacturing PMI remained in contraction but showed signs of gradual recovery, while the non-manufacturing PMI recorded modest growth, reflecting China’s continued efforts to stimulate domestic demand and support its sluggish economy.

Markets are now eyeing key economic data due next week, particularly inflation and trade figures.

Meanwhile, attention is also shifting to this month’s Politburo meeting, where investors anticipate new stimulus measures amid persistent economic headwinds such as looming US tariffs.

data.title: Siemens Restores Chinese Customer Access After US Lifts Curbs
data.description:
Siemens and other major chip design software companies were informed by the US that they no longer need government export licenses for business in China, Bloomberg News reported Thursday, citing statements.

The US Commerce Department lifted the requirement after the country signed a new trade deal with China. The German technology conglomerate told the news outlet that it has restored full access to its software and technology for Chinese customers.

data.title: BoJ Should Hike Rates After U.S. Tariff Impact: Board Member Takata
data.description:
The Bank of Japan should resume rate hikes after a temporary pause to assess the impact of U.S. tariffs on Japan’s economy, board member Hajime Takata said in a speech Thursday. “My view is that the BoJ is currently only pausing its policy interest rate hike cycle,” he noted, adding the bank should shift away from its ultra-loose policy after a period of "wait and see".

Takata said Japan is nearing the 2% inflation target, helped by strong corporate earnings, labor shortages, and rising wages.

While he expects this outlook to remain “broadly” unchanged despite President Trump’s sweeping tariff announcement on April 1, Takata stressed the need to monitor risks. “Given uncertainties regarding various U.S. policies remain high, the BoJ must conduct monetary policy in a more flexible manner without being too pessimistic,” he said.

He also warned that if the Fed resumes rate cuts, it could reduce the BoJ’s policy flexibility.

Still, he sees no signs of a U.S. recession akin to past crises.

data.title: Eurocommercial Closes Refinancing of Swedish, Italian Green Loans
data.description:
Eurocommercial Properties finalized the refinancing of two green loans in Sweden and Italy in line with its green finance framework, according to a Wednesday release.

The Dutch property investor increased the amount of a green loan on its assets in Sweden to 2.4 billion kronor from 1.8 billion kronor and extended its maturity date by three years to 2030 from 2027. The loan was renewed with Nordea Bank, filial i Sverige.

In addition, the company secured a green and sustainability-linked loan worth 200 million euros from ING Bank, Milan Branch, BNP Paribas - Succursale Italiana, and Banco BPM in Italy. The loan has a five-year term.

With the refinancing of loans due in 2025 now complete, the company is in talks to refinance remaining loans maturing in the second half of 2026 and anticipates finalizing them later in 2025.

data.title: Mirait One Buys Back 257,500 Shares in June for 641 Million Yen
data.description:
Mirait One repurchased 257,500 shares for 641 million yen through market transactions on the Tokyo Stock Exchange between June 1 and June 30, according to a Thursday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The buyback is part of a program approved on May 13 to acquire up to 2 million shares, or 2.2% of outstanding stock, for up to 3 billion yen through Sept.30.

data.title: Anax Metals Withdraws Arbitration Against Investor; Shares Up 17%
data.description:
Anax Metals has withdrawn its arbitration proceedings against a cornerstone investor over a disputed marketing agreement, confirming the convertible note remains valid and no default has occurred, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

On June 24, the company filed for arbitration against a cornerstone investor over unresolved exclusive marketing rights tied to a convertible note deal, the filing said.

However, the company confirmed that no default has occurred and the note remains in place, with both parties agreeing to defer any redemption or conversion until just before its Nov. 18, 2026, maturity, the filing added.

The company's shares surged almost 17% in recent Thursday trade.
